claim: Cross-chip memory becomes a moat only when the lab can retain which lessons survived domain shift.
mechanism: Many systems can store local notes, but fewer can track whether a tactic worked in more than one domain and under what boundaries it stopped working. The lab's opportunity is to encode that cross-chip survival data into packets and doctrine, which increases the reliability of future transfer recommendations and makes the memory more defensible than a raw archive.
boundary: This moat depends on actual cross-chip evidence. It cannot be claimed from one repo's local stabilization alone.
